label: 249_phillips curve_phillips_curve_slope
description: The Phillips curve describes how inflation is influenced by economic activity levels and expected future inflation. Central bank credibility in anchoring these expectations affects the relationship, as seen in discussions from economists like Christopher J. Waller.

earliest_articles.title: Inflation: Mind the Gap
earliest_articles.excerpt: Since the fourth quarter of 2007, inflation has declined substantially. During the same period, the unemployment ratehas more than doubled. earliest_articles.title: Modeling Inflation After the Crisis
earliest_articles.excerpt: Modeling Inflation After the Crisis In the United States, the rate of price inflation falls in recessions. Turning this observation into a useful inflation forecasting equation is difficult because of multiple sources of time variation in the inflation process, including changes in Fed policy and credibility. We…

earliest_articles.excerpt: The Phillips Curve is Alive and Well: Inflation and the NAIRU During the Slow Recovery The Phillips Curve (hereafter PC) is widely viewed as dead, destined to the mortuary scrapyard of discarded economic ideas. central_articles.title: Return of the Original Phillips Curve
central_articles.excerpt: The Phillips curve is a key mathematical relationship that many economists use to explain the behavior of inflation. The relationship presumes that inflation is partly driven by gap variables, which measure how much economic activity deviates from its potential, that is, the level of activity consistent with full use…

central_articles.excerpt: One way to think about factors that affect inflation is a mathematical relationship called the Phillips curve; it states that inflation is influenced by the level of economic activity and the inflation rate people expect for the future.
